1: A type: the second stone tablets — covenant renewal after apostasy. Jeremiah 31:31-34; Hebrews 8:8-12 (the new covenant written on hearts, not stone). 2 Corinthians 3:3: 'written not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts by the Spirit of the living God.' The broken law is renewed; but the new covenant internalizes it.
2: A theme: Moses to present himself at Sinai's summit in the morning. Morning as the time of covenant encounter: the Resurrection at dawn (Mark 16:2; John 20:1) as the morning that supersedes all prior Sinai mornings. Revelation 22:16: Christ as 'the bright morning star.'
3: A type: no one to accompany Moses; the mediator goes alone into God's presence. Hebrews 9:7,11-12: the high priest entered the Most Holy Place alone; Christ entered the heavenly sanctuary once for all, alone, with his own blood — no accompanying intercession needed or permitted.
4: A type: Moses cut the tablets and rose early in the morning to go up. A type of Christ's willing, early-rising obedience: Mark 1:35 (Jesus rises before dawn for prayer); Hebrews 12:2 'who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising the shame' — going toward God's presence without hesitation.
5: A type: YHWH descended in the cloud and stood with Moses; he proclaimed the name YHWH. John 1:14: 'the Word became flesh and eskenos among us' — the divine condescension at Sinai prefigures the permanent, final descent of God into human flesh at the incarnation.
6: A direct type: 'YHWH, YHWH, the compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in hesed and emet (steadfast love and faithfulness).' John 1:14: 'full of grace and truth (charis kai aletheia)' is the NT rendering of hesed w'emet. John 1:17: 'grace and truth came through Jesus Christ.' This is the foundational OT self-proclamation of God's character that Christ personally embodies — the most direct OT-NT verbal echo in the Gospel of John.
7: A revelation of God: 'keeping hesed for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin, but who will by no means clear the guilty.' The tension between mercy and justice that the cross alone resolves. Romans 3:25-26: God presented Christ as a hilasterion so that he might be 'just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus' — Ex 34:7 is satisfied on both sides at Calvary.
8: A theme: Moses bowed his head to the ground and worshiped. The fitting response to the divine name's proclamation is prostration in worship. Revelation 5:8-14: all creation falls down before the Lamb who embodies the character of Ex Exodus 34:6-7 perfectly.
9: A type: Moses intercedes for the stiff-necked people to be pardoned and taken as God's inheritance. Hebrews 7:25: 'he always lives to make intercession for them.' John 17:9-26: Christ's High Priestly Prayer claims the same ground — 'they are yours... I am asking on their behalf.'
10: A type: 'I am making a covenant... I will do wonders (nipla'ot) never done before in all the earth.' Covenant renewal with fresh divine wonders. Hebrews 8:6-13: the new covenant is 'better, enacted on better promises.' The wonders of Exodus are surpassed by the resurrection — the greatest wonder in all the earth (Acts 2:22-24; 1 Corinthians 15:20-22).
11: A type: YHWH commands the expulsion of the Canaanites before Israel. Colossians 2:15: 'having disarmed the rulers and authorities, he put them to open shame, triumphing over them in him.' Christ expels the hostile spiritual powers that oppose God's covenant people.
12: A theme: 'Take care, lest you make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land.' The danger of spiritual compromise with what is under divine judgment. 2 Corinthians 6:14-16: 'Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers... what accord has Christ with Belial?' The new covenant community's distinctiveness is non-negotiable.
13: A shadow: 'You shall tear down their altars and break their pillars.' John 2:14-17: the cleansing of the temple — Christ's covenant confrontation with compromised worship. Zeal for God's house (Psalms 69:9; John 2:17) is the characteristic mark of the true covenant keeper.
14: A revelation of God: 'You shall not worship any other god, for YHWH — his name is Jealous — is a jealous God.' Divine jealousy as covenant commitment to his people's ultimate welfare. 2 Corinthians 11:2: Paul's 'godly jealousy'; John 14:6 and Rev 2-3: the exclusive claim of Christ is the new covenant form of this fidelity.
15: A theme: making covenants with pagan nations leads to spiritual unfaithfulness. The language of spiritual adultery applies to the church (James 4:4; Rev 2-3 letters to the seven churches). The church's exclusive loyalty is to Christ as bridegroom (2 Corinthians 11:2; Revelation 19:7-9).
16: A theme: intermarriage with pagans draws Israel into their gods. 2 Corinthians 6:14: 'Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers.' The covenant community maintains its distinctiveness because it is betrothed to Christ (Ephesians 5:25-27; Revelation 21:2).
17: A theme: 'You shall not make for yourself molten gods.' The continuing prohibition against self-made worship objects. 1 John 5:21: 'Little children, keep yourselves from idols.' Colossians 3:5: covetousness is idolatry. The prohibition extends beyond images to anything that displaces the centrality of Christ.
18: A type: keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread. 1 Corinthians 5:7-8: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed. Therefore let us keep the feast, not with old leaven of malice and evil, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.' The feast finds its fulfillment in the ongoing life of the new covenant community.
19: A direct type: 'All the firstborn males are mine.' Luke 2:22-24: Mary and Joseph present Jesus at the temple in direct fulfillment of this law. Colossians 1:15,18: Christ as 'the firstborn over all creation' and 'the firstborn from the dead' — the one to whom all the firstborn legislation ultimately points.
20: A type: redeem the firstborn donkey with a lamb; redeem your firstborn sons. 1 Peter 1:18-19: 'redeemed... not with perishable things like silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or defect.' Galatians 3:13: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law.' Every redemption price in the OT is a shadow of his.
21: A shadow: the Sabbath command embedded in covenant renewal. Colossians 2:16-17: the sabbath is a shadow of things to come; the substance belongs to Christ. Hebrews 4:9-11: 'there remains a sabbath rest for the people of God... let us strive to enter that rest.' Christ's finished work ('It is finished' — John 19:30) is its basis.
22: A type: the Feast of Weeks (Shavuot/Pentecost) and the Feast of Ingathering. Acts 2 fulfills the Feast of Weeks — the outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost is the first-fruits harvest of the new covenant. The Feast of Ingathering at year's end points to the eschatological harvest (Revelation 14:14-16; Matthew 13:39).
23: A theme: three times a year all males shall appear before YHWH. The required access to God's presence as covenant privilege. Hebrews 10:19-22: 'we have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place by the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way.' The obligation becomes a gift — free and perpetual access through Christ.
24: A theme: YHWH will enlarge the territory and protect the people while they go up. Hebrews 13:6: 'The Lord is my helper; I will not fear. What can man do to me?' The covenant community's vulnerability during worship is covered by divine protection. 1 Peter 5:7: 'Cast all your anxiety on him because he cares for you.'
25: A shadow: blood sacrifice not offered with leavened bread; Passover lamb not to remain overnight. Hebrews 9:14: Christ 'through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God' — the true Passover sacrifice is perfect, complete, and unrepeatable. 1 Corinthians 5:7: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.'
26: A direct type: 'The best of the first-fruits of your ground you shall bring to the house of YHWH your God.' 1 Corinthians 15:20,23: 'Christ has been raised from the dead, the first-fruits of those who have fallen asleep... Christ the first-fruits, then at his coming those who belong to Christ.' The first-fruits law directly encodes the resurrection pattern.
27: A type: 'Write these words, for in accordance with these words I have made a covenant with you and with Israel.' The written covenant. 2 Corinthians 3:3: the new covenant is 'written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts.' The external writing gives way to the internal.
28: A type: Moses was with YHWH forty days and forty nights without eating or drinking; he wrote the Ten Words. Jesus's forty-day wilderness fast (Matthew 4:1-2) recapitulates Moses's forty days. The true Israel, filled with the Spirit, succeeds where Israel repeatedly fails; his forty days end in victory, not apostasy.
29: A type: Moses came down from Sinai, his face shining because he had been speaking with God. The Transfiguration (Matthew 17:2 'his face shone like the sun') is the NT fulfillment. But Moses's glory was reflected and fading; Christ's is intrinsic and permanent (Hebrews 1:3; 2 Corinthians 3:7-11). The glory Moses reflected Christ possessed from eternity.
30: A type: Aaron and all Israel saw Moses's shining face and were afraid to approach. Hebrews 12:19-21: the terrifying appearances of the old covenant were such that even Moses said 'I tremble with fear.' Matthew 17:6-7: at the Transfiguration the disciples fell face-down in terror — but Jesus touched them and said 'Rise, and have no fear.' The new covenant replaces Sinai's terror with welcome.
31: A theme: Moses called to Aaron and the leaders; they returned to him. The mediator summons the community into God's presence after the terrifying display. Matthew 11:28: 'Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest.' Christ's summons issues after the display of who he is, not in spite of it.
32: A type: Moses gave all the commands YHWH had spoken on Sinai. John 1:17: 'the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ.' Moses conveys the commandments; Christ fulfills and embodies them (Matthew 5:17; Romans 13:10; 2 Corinthians 1:20).
33: A direct type: after speaking to the Israelites Moses put a veil (masveh) over his face. 2 Corinthians 3:13: 'Moses put a veil over his face so that the Israelites might not gaze at the outcome of what was being brought to an end.' The veil conceals the fading, provisional character of the Mosaic covenant — its glory real but temporary.
34: A direct type: when Moses went before YHWH he removed the veil. 2 Corinthians 3:16: 'when one turns to the Lord, the veil is removed.' In Paul's argument 'the Lord' is identified with the Spirit (v17) and with Christ (1 Corinthians 15:45; 2 Corinthians 3:17) — turning to Christ removes what the veil represents: the opacity of the old covenant.
35: A direct type: the Israelites saw Moses's face shining; he put the veil back until he went before YHWH again. 2 Corinthians 3:18: 'We all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another — this comes from the Lord who is the Spirit.' The new covenant removes the veil permanently; ongoing transformation into Christ's image is the result.
